No one has ever hardened their heart against God and prospered. God is the joy of his people, so let them rejoice when they come before him. He who derives being from no one but gives being to all is obligated by promise and covenant to bless his people. He is to be praised as a God of mercy and tender compassion. He always cares for those who are afflicted and oppressed; repentant sinners, who are helpless and exposed more than any fatherless children, are welcomed into his family and share in all their blessings.

New mercies should remind us of former mercies. If God leads his people into a wilderness, he will surely go before them and bring them out of it. He provided for them both in the wilderness and in Canaan. The daily manna seems to be meant here. It points to the spiritual provision for God’s Israel. The Spirit of grace and the gospel of grace are the plentiful rain with which God confirms his inheritance, and from which their fruit is found. Christ will come as showers that water the earth. The account of Israel's victories should be applied to the victories over death and hell by the exalted Redeemer for those who are his. Israel in Egypt among the kilns appeared wretched, but possessing Canaan during the reigns of David and Solomon appeared glorious. Thus, slaves of Satan, when converted to Christ, when justified and sanctified by him, appear honorable. When they reach heaven, all remains of their sinful state will disappear; they will be as the wings of the dove, covered with silver, and her feathers as gold. Complete salvation will render those white as snow who were vile and loathsome because of the guilt and defilement of sin.

The ascension of Christ must be meant here, and it is applied to that event. He received, as the price of his death, the gifts needed for the conversion of sinners and the salvation of believers. He continually bestows these gifts even on rebellious people, so that the Lord God might dwell among them as their Friend and Father. He gave gifts to people. Having received power to give eternal life, Jesus gives it to as many as were given him. Christ came to a rebellious world not to condemn it but so that through him it might be saved. The glory of Zion’s King is that he is a Savior and Benefactor to all his willing people and a consuming fire to all who persist in rebellion against him. So numerous, so weighty are the gifts of God's generosity that he may truly be said to load us with them. He will not satisfy us with present things as a portion but will be the God of our salvation. Jesus has authority and power to rescue his people from the dominion of death by taking away its sting when they die and giving them complete victory over it when they rise again. The crown of the head, the chief pride and glory of the enemy, will be struck; Christ will crush the head of the serpent.

The victories with which God blessed David over Israel's enemies are types of Christ’s victory, for himself and for all believers. Those who take him as theirs may see him acting as their God, as their King, for their good, and in answer to their prayers; especially in and by his word and ordinances. The kingdom of the Messiah will be submitted to by all rulers and learned people in the world. The people seem to address the king, verse #. But the words are applicable to the Redeemer, to his church, and every true believer. We pray that you, O God the Son, will complete your work for us by finishing your good work in us.

A powerful invitation is given to those who are outside to join the church. Some will submit from fear; overcome by their consciences and the warnings of Providence, they are brought to make peace with the church. Others will submit willingly, verse #. There is beauty and benefit in the service of God and in the gospel of Christ which went forth from Jerusalem, enough to invite sinners out of all nations.

God is to be admired and adored with reverence and godly fear by all who attend in his holy places. The God of Israel gives strength and power to his people. Through Christ strengthening us we can do all things, not otherwise; therefore he must have the glory of all we do, along with our humble thanks for enabling us to do it, and for accepting the work of his hands in us.
